summary: This mock interview focuses on designing Reddit's homepage feed. The employee, Kevin, starts by clarifying requirements like daily active users (50 million), number of posts per page (25), post types (text and image), and focusing on logged-in users and top posts from subscribed subreddits within 24 hours. He then moves to calculations, estimating data size per post and proposing optimizations like using thumbnails to reduce bandwidth. The high-level design involves a microservice architecture with web, app, ranking, feed, user, and post services, along with databases and an object store. Key design considerations include horizontal scalability with NoSQL, sharding strategies (initially by post ID, then refined to subreddit ID), and avoiding stale data through on-demand generation, caching, and a crawler for active subreddits.

turns.text: Yes. All right. And from what I remember, on Reddit, there are several tabs at the very top where you can sort by different settings, like hot, top, controversial. Should we care about all these different post or feed sorting methods?

Only posts from your subscribed subreddits should appear on your homepage. I think that was the behavior. Should we care about building this kind of homepage feed for logged-in users, or is it fine to just do anonymous users?

And let's see what else. Maybe a feed service that would be in charge of creating the Reddit feed for a user, a user service that would be in charge of keeping track of updating and getting data from the user database, as well as a, let's see, a post service as well. Let's do for this one. And we would also need an object store to store all the different images as well. Yeah, I think that's pretty much it for the higher-level design. I have some thoughts about the post database as well.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE

I'm thinking we would probably need some horizontal scalability, or else it might not be possible to fit, I'm guessing, hundreds of millions of posts on Reddit into this database. In that case, I'm also thinking of something like a NoSQL server, because when displaying your Reddit feed on the homepage, it's probably not as important that everything you see is the freshest or most up-to-date or most consistent. So, a NoSQL server, and we should have some horizontal scalability, and I think we should scale it in terms of sharding. So we can probably shard or distribute the posts across multiple database shards based on their post ID. So, shard by post ID. And that would allow us to kind of have an equal amount of data across all of our shards.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE

If the post ID is just an integer value, and the post ID is an auto-generated number, we could just use something like modulo to distribute this post to that shard, or to this other shard. If you have 100 shards, then it would be something like the post ID mod 100. Okay, we belong to that shard. However, usually, modulo sharding, using something simple like modulo, is not a good solution if you ever need to repartition your database, if you need to add a new shard to this database, like if each of your shards are getting too big or something. In that case, it would kind of shift all the modular values, like modding by 100, modding by 101, that results in totally different values. So, I know there's something called consistent hashing we can do to avoid having to do that, so we can increase the number of shards without having to repartition a lot of our data. Yeah, yeah. Okay, cut that. Are you probably out there? Just pause for a little bit.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, sure. Yeah, okay. And okay, so I think that strategy makes sense just to account for when your shards get too big, and you have a strategy to deal with that if that happens. Okay, all right, yeah. All right, sounds good.

Oh yeah, I'm also missing load balancers, but I definitely want them to be in here. So let me just, I can just make this triangle. And right, we want to have load balancers in front of the web server and in front of the app server as well. And let's see. Yeah, I feel like a service would probably consist of multiple servers. Honestly, I think there should be load balancers in front of most of these lines, most of these arrows also, just because we don't want the system to have a single source of failure, where one server goes down, the entire Reddit homepage can be loaded. Yeah. This is kind of the overall design.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so how are you going to be calculating the top posts with this system?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, so what I'm thinking of is, the app servers, when the client makes the request to the Reddit homepage, it will go to web servers, and then the app servers will kind of pull the data from the various other services. It would talk to the user service, determining what subreddits this user is subscribed to, and it would talk to the feed service, passing in those subreddits. And the feed service should generate, should pull posts appropriately for those subreddits back, and the feed service should rank them appropriately, and then give them back to the client, basically. Okay, yeah. I'm thinking with this flow, in order to pull the posts across different subreddits, because we are sharding the post database by post ID, we would basically have to query every single shard to get the different posts across all the subreddits. Yeah, which might be slightly taxing, and maybe there's a better way to do it. Perhaps, instead of sharding by post ID, we can shard by what subreddit that particular post belongs to.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, that sounds reasonable.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE

And that way, say a user is only subscribed to a few subreddits, like one of them being funny, or let's say he's only subscribed to funny, then we would just have to only query that post database from the feed service instead of querying all the different shards. Yeah. Okay, so let me just update that: shard by subreddit ID. A problem with sharding by subreddit is that some subreddits have a lot more posts than other subreddits, and that can result in some shards being much larger than other shards. Yeah. So, I think in that case, maybe we can just have some functionality where if a shard gets too big, we can expand it to two different shards, we can break it up. And then with our consistent hashing that we talked about earlier, it wouldn't be too difficult to move some of that data from that shard onto the new shard.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, that sounds good. How would you be avoiding stale data?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE

And then how would you make sure that you are constantly giving back, if the user is visiting Reddit five times a day, each time I'm assuming that those 25 posts are going to have to be fresh, right? You mean fresh as in they should be different from one another, or if there are changes to what the top 25 posts are for this user, then yes, they should be different.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, gotcha, I got you, I see what you mean. Yeah, so I think the easy solution for this would just be to generate the feed on demand. Basically, when the user visits Reddit, we would, at that point in time, have the feed service call the post service, get the latest 24-hour posts, and then rank them accordingly, send them back to the user. Yeah, by doing that generation on demand, it will ensure that the user will have no stale data, but it would also mean that that might be kind of taxing for our backend to do that, given all the number of pages that we have. Yeah, so I'm thinking that we could possibly implement it in a better way. Let's see, we could have, just off the top of my head, I'm thinking of having a cache. So maybe when a user visits Reddit for the first time that day, if the user visits Reddit for the first time that day, then we would do this online generation of their feed and then cache that result. If the user visits in the next minute or two, we can just serve that feed from the cache.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so do you have any strategies to not tax the backend so much?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Right, so I'm thinking the first thing is that we probably don't have to look at all the posts in the post database. Maybe we can cache the top posts per subreddit, and that would allow us to, on a daily basis or every couple hours, when a user makes a request to Reddit's homepage, we can just fetch those top posts from all the subreddits that they're subscribed to, instead of looking at the entire post database across all the hundreds of millions of posts, and just rank those top posts accordingly. Okay. And we could have a crawler that would do this kind of caching, and this crawler could crawl every couple hours or so per subreddit. Actually, just a quick question, do you know how many subreddits there are on Reddit?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Let's assume for our purposes around two million, but I also would say not all of them are active at the same time, so I'll assume like 130,000 or so are active.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, okay, gotcha. Right, so that's really important to know that only a small portion of all subreddits are active subreddits. So maybe we can just do crawling on these active subreddits, crawl the top posts in the last 24 hours of these active subreddits, and keep track of those. And then for non-active subreddits, we can maybe just, if not active subreddits wouldn't have any new posts, we can just exclude them from being fetched for our homepage purposes.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And how are you differentiating between whether a subreddit is active or not?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I'm thinking we could have another layer of crawling, I guess. For this one, maybe once every day, at a much slower cadence, that checks, 'Does this subreddit have any new posts?' If it does have a new post, then we can check this subreddit as active, we can move it to the active subreddit category. Otherwise, it would just be considered non-active. Yeah, so having that cache, we can add that in right here, then remove these two top posts cache, and we would have a crawler that populates this cache from the post service. Okay, okay. Yeah, I think this is pretty much it for the design then. Do you have any other questions?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I think this is a good place to stop. I want to ask you, Kevin, what do you think is the most challenging part about approaching questions like this?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think just keeping track of all the different components of the system, keeping track of all the different requirements, that is pretty challenging. But I think the more system design you do, the more experience you have, the better over time.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And the devil is in the details for system design. So, I think you did a good job in kind of highlighting the little things that you wanted to add to this system, starting from the load balancers that you addressed right from the beginning, the sharding strategy that you wanted to use, and then caching at the end. So it's these little things that you kind of add to a system that convey to an interviewer that you've thought about this, and you know what you're talking about, and you have ideas for how to design a really robust system. Thank you. Yeah, thanks so much for being with us today, Kevin. This was great, and I hope you build the next Reddit, and good luck with your upcoming interviews. Thanks so much for watching. Don't forget to hit the like and subscribe buttons below to let us know that this video is valuable for you, and of course, check out hundreds more videos just like this at tryexponent.com. Thanks for watching and good luck on your upcoming interview.

training_pairs.question: How would you design Reddit's homepage feed?
training_pairs.answer: Gotcha, gotcha. Reddit's homepage feed, like just kind of like the first page that you see when you visit Reddit?
training_pairs.follow_up: Yes, exactly. Take a second to think about it.

training_pairs.question: Can you tell me first of all, how many daily users are on Reddit?
training_pairs.follow_up: From what I know about Reddit's homepage, there are a certain number of posts, I'm guessing from various subreddits, right?

training_pairs.question: Should we design for all these different post types?
training_pairs.answer: I think let's focus on text and images for now.

training_pairs.question: Should we care about all these different post or feed sorting methods?
training_pairs.answer: Let's not worry too much about the ranking for now. Let's just pretend that whatever posts you see are the top posts of the subreddits that the logged-in users subscribed to in the past 24 hours.

training_pairs.question: Should we care about building this kind of homepage feed for logged-in users, or is it fine to just do anonymous users?
How are you assuming the 300 characters value?
training_pairs.answer: I'm just thinking from my experience visiting Reddit and clicking on text posts, 300 characters is kind of enough for one or two small paragraphs.
training_pairs.follow_up: And is this like an excerpt that you would see of a post, or is it the entire contents?

training_pairs.question: Is there anything that you can do to reduce this number off the bat?
training_pairs.answer: I think first we should think about how to reduce this value for a single post. If we have to load six billion posts a day, and each post is 50 kilobytes on average, that would be pretty significant and probably would not be good for bandwidth, outgoing bandwidth. So perhaps what we can do is instead of sending a full-size image for every single post on the homepage initially, we can send that big image on demand once the user decides to enlarge it. But by default, we can just send a smaller thumbnail.
training_pairs.follow_up: How's that going to affect your calculation?

training_pairs.question: How's that going to affect your calculation?
training_pairs.answer: If we change it to a thumbnail, maybe like 10 kilobytes, just a low-resolution image, that would probably reduce it by 10x as well. So that each image post, or each post on average, would be five kilobytes instead of 50 kilobytes. Although, another additional metadata we would need to have would be a link to that full-size image in our object store.
training_pairs.follow_up: Cool, cool. And I assume that the size for that would be similar to the other hyperlink, right?

training_pairs.question: I think this sounds reasonable. So you have a sense for how big each post would be and just how much bandwidth you're going to need if you're loading 25 posts every time someone visits the homepage, right? So this sounds good. Let's move on to the high-level design.
training_pairs.answer: I'm thinking that there will probably be quite a few services involved in loading the homepage, and it might be good to split our system design up into a microservice architecture, where we have multiple different services, different servers talking to one another. And that would just be better in general for scalability, fault tolerance, and also separation of concerns between these different services, like building them. So as for the different services we would need for this homepage, I'm thinking just the default web server, a basic web server that talks to the client, some application servers that handle the different backend logic, maybe a ranking service that would be in charge of ranking the different posts, a feed service that would be in charge of creating the Reddit feed for a user, a user service that would be in charge of keeping track of updating and getting data from the user database, as well as a post service. As for databases, we would need a database and an object store to store all the different images as well. I have some thoughts about the post database as well.
training_pairs.follow_up: What are your thoughts on that?

training_pairs.question: What's the criteria for sharding here? What's the criteria?
training_pairs.answer: I was just thinking of a simple formula. If the post ID is just an integer value, and the post ID is an auto-generated number, we could just use something like modulo to distribute this post to that shard, or to this other shard. If you have 100 shards, then it would be something like the post ID mod 100. However, usually, modulo sharding is not a good solution if you ever need to repartition your database, if you need to add a new shard to this database, like if each of your shards are getting too big or something. In that case, it would kind of shift all the modular values. So, I know there's something called consistent hashing we can do to avoid having to do that, so we can increase the number of shards without having to repartition a lot of our data.

training_pairs.question: Okay, so how are you going to be calculating the top posts with this system?
training_pairs.answer: The app servers, when the client makes the request to the Reddit homepage, it will go to web servers, and then the app servers will pull the data from the various other services. It would talk to the user service, determining what subreddits this user is subscribed to, and it would talk to the feed service, passing in those subreddits. And the feed service should generate, should pull posts appropriately for those subreddits back, and the feed service should rank them appropriately, and then give them back to the client, basically. I'm thinking with this flow, in order to pull the posts across different subreddits, because we are sharding the post database by post ID, we would basically have to query every single shard to get the different posts across all the subreddits. Which might be slightly taxing, and maybe there's a better way to do it. Perhaps, instead of sharding by post ID, we can shard by what subreddit that particular post belongs to.

training_pairs.question: How would you be avoiding stale data?
training_pairs.answer: I think the easy solution for this would just be to generate the feed on demand. Basically, when the user visits Reddit, we would, at that point in time, have the feed service call the post service, get the latest 24-hour posts, and then rank them accordingly, send them back to the user. By doing that generation on demand, it will ensure that the user will have no stale data, but it would also mean that that might be kind of taxing for our backend to do that, given all the number of pages that we have. So I'm thinking that we could possibly implement it in a better way. We could have, just off the top of my head, I'm thinking of having a cache. So maybe when a user visits Reddit for the first time that day, then we would do this online generation of their feed and then cache that result. If the user visits in the next minute or two, we can just serve that feed from the cache.
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, so do you have any strategies to not tax the backend so much?

training_pairs.question: And how are you differentiating between whether a subreddit is active or not?
training_pairs.answer: I'm thinking we could have another layer of crawling. For this one, maybe once every day, at a much slower cadence, that checks, 'Does this subreddit have any new posts?' If it does have a new post, then we can check this subreddit as active, we can move it to the active subreddit category. Otherwise, it would just be considered non-active. So having that cache, we can add that in right here, then remove these two top posts cache, and we would have a crawler that populates this cache from the post service.

training_pairs.question: What do you think is the most challenging part about approaching questions like this?
training_pairs.answer: I think just keeping track of all the different components of the system, keeping track of all the different requirements, that is pretty challenging. But I think the more system design you do, the more experience you have, the better over time.
